JavaScript Core Concepts (2): LHS vs RHS — What Those Errors Actually Mean

If you've spent any time in JavaScript, you've probably bumped into the terms LHS and RHS. They show up a lot, and honestly they're pretty simple once you get the idea — they're all about assigning values and looking values up.
The Left-Hand Side basically means you're assigning a value to the variable on the left. The simplest example is
— you're putting the number 1 into the variable
So when does an LHS error happen?
It happens when whatever's on the left isn't something JavaScript can actually assign to. For example, trying to assign to a string:
Type that into your browser DevTools and you'll get
SyntaxError: invalid assignment left-hand side debugger eval code:1
using the parser from the previous post.
Now here's a weird one. What if you try the same thing but with a variable declaration? Like this:
This is where it gets interesting — you might not see the "Left-Hand Side" error message at all. On the browser I was testing with, Chrome 80.0.3987.149, you get
instead. The reason the wording differs is that each browser's execution environment phrases its errors differently. Which tells you something important — the execution environment really matters.
The LHS error was kind of hard to pin down. What about RHS? RHS stands for Right-Hand Side.
RHS means you're reading a value from the variable on the right. What does that mean exactly? Let's look at some code.
on the right and shows it in DevTools.
Another way to put it: RHS is about referencing or looking up a value. The example below is actually an RHS lookup. First there's an LHS assignment, and then when we hit
, it looks up and references the value of

var a = '小明';
var b = a;
The RHS error message is a little different from LHS, though. RHS doesn't directly say "RHS error" — it shows you something more like
And again, the exact message varies slightly by browser:
VM226:1 Uncaught ReferenceError: a is not defined at <anonymous>:1:13
, it means that variable hasn't been defined yet, so it doesn't exist.
If you hit any of the errors above, I'd strongly suggest fixing them, because your code won't run properly otherwise — the moment JavaScript hits an error, it can't keep executing past that point.

You'll run into LHS and RHS all over JavaScript — they're tied to assigning values and looking values up. This post breaks down what LHS and RHS mean, when each one throws an error, and why the exact wording of those errors changes from browser to browser.
